items.correct: A small integrated chip containing CPU, memory, and I/O for controlling a device.
items.misconception: A very small computer like a laptop.
items.random_wrong: A remote control for a TV.
items.why: Students think microcontrollers are mini-PCs. They are specialised: limited resources, designed for a single embedded task. No operating system needed - just dedicated code running directly.

items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.q: If voltage is doubled and resistance is unchanged, what happens to the current?
items.correct: Current doubles (V = IR, so I = V/R).
items.why: Students get the direction of proportionality wrong. Current is PROPORTIONAL to voltage (when R is constant) - more push, more flow. They sometimes confuse this with current being inversely proportional to resistance.
It's 2005. “1995” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Arduino Uno's analogue input ADC has how many bits?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 10. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Arduino Uno's 10-bit ADC has how many discrete levels (0 to 2^10 - 1)?
items.why: The real figure is 1024. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
